exemplar.title: How does urban traffic noise pollution (in decibels) in northeast Beijing, China affect the density of Oriental magpie (Pica serica) communities (in birds per 100m)?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does urban traffic noise pollution (in decibels) in northeast Beijing, China affect the density of Oriental magpie (Pica serica) communities (in birds per 100m)?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report has a clear, consistent structure with numbered sections and headings.
Terminology (LAeq, Pearsonâs r, density) is used correctly.
Overall flow is logical and coherent, enabling reader comprehension.
weaknesses: Prose is occasionally verbose and lacks concision.
Some figures (e.g. map) need scale bars and north arrows for clarity.
Table and reference formatting contain minor inconsistencies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and logical flow meet the top band, but verbosity prevents full concision. The work demonstrates either consistent terminology or concision, but not both fully, placing COM in band 2 (score 2/3).

strengths: Research question is relevant, coherent, narrowly focused and measurable.
Provides a thorough discussion of noise pollution at both global and local scales.
Clearly explains connections between noise levels and magpie density with literature support.
weaknesses: Spatial extent (ânortheast Beijingâ) remains slightly imprecise.
Could integrate more speciesâspecific ecological impacts to strengthen context.
No explicit causal pathway diagram to illustrate connections.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently placed CXT in the 5â6 band but noted minor imprecision in spatial definition and occasional tangential data. A score of 5 reflects the highâquality, focused question and comprehensive context, while leaving room for a perfectly defined spatial scope.

strengths: Method is repeatable and would yield relevant data.
Site categories are described clearly with maps and tables.
Risks and ethics are identified and basic mitigations outlined.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks justification of radius choice and statistical power.
Risk assessment is only outlined, lacking a formal matrix or detailed likelihood/severity ratings.
Hypothesis is not explicitly stated to guide analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the procedure is methodologically sound (band 3â4), sampling strategy and ethics are only described, not justified comprehensively (band 3), and the missing formal risk matrix and hypothesis statement limit topâband performance. Hence PLA is awarded 3/6.

strengths: Methodological strengths and weaknesses are identified in detail.
Proposed improvements (additional observers, longer temporal scope) are substantive and address major weaknesses.
Discussion relates findings to prior studies and acknowledges limits on causation.
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusions omits deeper consideration of spatial scale and confounding variables.
Could integrate quantitative estimates of how much leaf cover or seasonality affect counts.
Broader socio-ecological implications are mentioned but not fully developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (method evaluation, improvements) clearly meet the top band, while conclusion evaluation falls in the midâband. A balanced score of 4/6 reflects this mixed performance.

strengths: All relevant data are presented in wellâlabelled tables and figures.
Data analysis correctly identifies patterns, uses means, SDs, and Pearsonâs r.
Conclusion is valid, wellâsupported by data, and appropriately cautious about causality.
weaknesses: Axis labels and font sizes in figures could be improved for readability.
Inferential depth could be enhanced by reporting p-values or confidence intervals.
Ecological mechanisms underlying observed patterns are only briefly suggested.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation, analysis, and conclusion all meet the top descriptor band, with minor cosmetic and inferential omissions preventing a perfect 6. A score of 5 accurately reflects this high level of performance.

strengths: Proposes a clear, actionable solution (low-emission zones) based directly on findings.
Justifies the application with reference to noise reduction literature.
Evaluates both strengths (systemic noise reduction) and weaknesses (economic cost) comprehensively.
weaknesses: Abrupt transition into the application section.
Could ground the proposal with a review of existing local policies and barriers.
Lacks a rough cost-benefit estimate or feasibility assessment.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both application proposal and evaluation strands meet the highest descriptor, warranting the maximum 3/3 for APP.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialist terms (LAeq, Pearson r, density) are used correctly and consistently; however, **wording is occasionally verbose and could be more succinct**, so the work meets âeither uses appropriate terminology or is conciseâ. Band 2 selected.

The discussion outlines how findings accord with prior studies and acknowledges inability to prove causation. **Evaluation is present but omits deeper consideration of spatial scale, confounding variables or broader socio-ecological implications**, leaving some gaps. This aligns with âevaluates â¦ with omissionsâ (3-4 band); midpoint awarded.

The conclusion restates the negative correlation, links to literature, and prudently avoids inferring causality, citing methodological limits. **Claims are directly supported by calculated r-values and consistent with presented figures**, satisfying the requirement for a valid, data-supported conclusion. Minor brevity on ecological mechanisms keeps the score at 5 rather than 6.

The student calculates means, standard deviations and Pearson r for each session, correctly interprets the sign and strength, and comments on variability. **Analysis addresses patterns fully and correctly.** Although significance testing is not performed, the descriptor emphasises completeness and correctness of pattern analysis, which is achieved; hence a 5.

The student proposes several substantive improvementsâadditional observers, winter sampling, longer temporal scopeâand explains how each addresses quantified weaknesses. **These modifications would significantly reduce major errors and extend research utility**, fulfilling the descriptor for significant modifications; hence 5 marks.

The procedureâpaired noise measurements and 5-min point counts at 10 sites over two datesâ**is repeatable and would yield relevant data**, demonstrated by consistent SDs and a usable data set.  
Nevertheless, limitations acknowledged by the student (single observer, only two sampling days, potential undercounting) mean the method may not collect *sufficient* data to establish a robust relationship across seasons. These weaknesses place it at the top of the 3-4 band rather than the 5-6 band.

The submission **explains, in several places, how the chosen species and measured variable connect to the broader issue**: effects of traffic noise on bird behaviour, Beijingâs high dB readings, and Oriental magpie adaptability.  
Links are built through literature synthesis, local expert quotations and justification for selecting specific urban categories, giving a comprehensive picture.  
Minor gaps (e.g. no explicit causal pathway diagram) prevent full marks, but the description clearly exceeds âdescribes with omissionsâ and aligns with the highest descriptor band.

**The research question is explicitly stated, narrowly focused and framed in measurable terms** (âHow does urban traffic noiseâ¦ affect the density of Oriental magpie communitiesâ¦ in birds per 100 m?â).  
The wording identifies the independent (urban traffic noise, quantified in dB) and dependent (magpie density) variables, specifies the study area (northeast Beijing) and the organism (Pica serica), leaving little ambiguity and allowing a clear pathway for data collection.  
Minor imprecision remains in defining the exact spatial extent (e.g. ânortheast Beijingâ could still cover many districts), so a perfect 6 is not awarded, but the question easily meets the descriptor for a ârelevant, coherent and focusedâ RQ.

Strengths (holistic benefits, systemic noise reduction) and weaknesses (economic cost, uncertain ecological magnitude) are discussed. **All relevant pros and cons are considered**, meeting the descriptor for a full evaluation; maximum 3 marks awarded.

The report is divided into numbered sections with consistent headings, tables and figures, enabling easy navigation. **Organisation is maintained from introduction to bibliography, fulfilling the âwell-structured throughoutâ requirement.**

Strengths (range of noise levels, low SD, effective key) and weaknesses (undercounting, leaf cover, seasonality) are **discussed in detail with clear explanations of how each impacts validity**. The breadth and depth fulfil the top-band descriptor; slight repetition keeps mark at 5 rather than 6.

The student **provides an extended discussion of noise pollution as a global and local environmental issue**, citing WHO limits, epidemiological data, and the particular exceedances documented in Beijing.  
The background also links urban expansion, transport growth and recorded dB levels, situating the problem solidly within environmental-systems discourse.  
Because the treatment touches ecological, health and policy dimensions rather than merely listing facts, it satisfies the âdiscussesâ level; occasional tangential statistics mean the depth is good but not exemplary, so 5/6 is appropriate.

Site selection is described (residential, commercial, transport hub, nature categories; two sites each; map and table supplied). **However, justification remains partial**âno explanation of why a 100 m radius or two visits is statistically adequate, and sample size rationale is not offered.  
Thus the student âdescribes the choiceâ without a thorough defence, matching the 3-4 band; mid-band score awarded.

A short risk section notes traffic proximity and exhaust inhalation, with mitigation (sidewalk positioning, masks). **Ethical considerations are mentioned only briefly (âlimitedâ) and no formal risk matrix is supplied.**  
This constitutes an outline rather than a comprehensive treatment, so the strand sits comfortably in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

All raw data are tabulated; means and SDs are provided; a clear scatter plot summarises both sessions. **No relevant data appear to be omitted, and visual formats are appropriate and labelled.** Minor aesthetic issues (small fonts in some images) prevent awarding the absolute maximum, but the work meets the descriptor for the top band.

A clear applicationâimplementation of low-emission zonesâis **explicitly justified by the studyâs finding that transport hubs show highest noise**, and linked to literature on dB reductions. This meets the highest band for this criterion.

Arguments progress logically from context to methods, results and evaluation, with smooth transitions and minimal redundancy. **The coherence enables the reader to follow reasoning without confusion**, matching the highest descriptor.

annotations.comment: Variable definitions are clear, but the hypothesis statement is missing; explicitly articulate a testable hypothesis (e.g. âMean magpie density decreases with increasing LAeq dBâ) to guide analysis.
The research question is focused and measurable; to enhance precision, specify the exact spatial extent (for instance the specific districts or coordinates) rather than ânortheast Beijingâ alone.

The background discussion effectively situates noise pollution globally and locally; consider adding specific ecological impacts on Pica serica to strengthen the link between general noise data and your focal species.

Justification for choosing Oriental magpie is clear and well referenced; you could further reinforce this by integrating local noise measurement data to directly connect species adaptability to measured dB levels.

Site categories (residential, commercial, hub, nature) demonstrate thoughtful variation in noise exposure; strengthen this by justifying the number of sites per category and statistical power of two replicates.
Table 1 effectively catalogs locations with geographic details; adding unique site codes would streamline cross-referencing between tables, text, and figures.
The density formula is presented, but it is unclear whether 100 m refers to radius or diameter; clarify the sampling area calculation (e.g. $$A=\pi r^2$$ for a 100 m radius) to avoid ambiguity.
The 100 m radius selection is noted, but the resulting 3.4 kmÂ² area calculation warrants verification; include a brief justification or calculation for that area to ensure accuracy.
Figure 1 is a useful spatial reference; for improved communication, include a scale bar and north arrow, and ensure map resolution remains clear when printed.
Selection of the NIOSH SLM app is appropriate; specify whether the app was calibrated against a reference instrument to ensure measurement validity.
The narrative describing Table 3 is clear and interprets variability; for inferential depth, include corresponding p-values or confidence intervals for consistency.
Interpretation of Pearson r values is accurate; to strengthen inferential analysis, report significance levels (e.g. p-values) alongside correlation coefficients.
In Figure 3, increase axis label font sizes and consider adding the regression line equation to improve interpretability.
Ethical considerations are briefly noted; develop a formal risk assessment table with likelihood and severity ratings, and include any necessary permits or approvals.
Introduce summary statistics (mean, SD) in the text before Table 2 to guide readers toward key patterns prior to reviewing the raw data.
The bird count and noise data in Table 1 are comprehensive; consider splitting this large table into two for clarity (e.g. counts vs. noise statistics) to improve readability.
Conclusions are data-supported and appropriately cautious; you might briefly propose an ecological mechanism explaining how noise influences magpie behavior.
Weaknesses are articulated thoroughly; you could strengthen the evaluation by estimating how leaf cover quantitatively suppresses counts, perhaps via repeat sampling off-season.
Bibliography formatting is consistent; verify that every in-text citation appears in the reference list and vice versa to ensure completeness.
The final reference entry is split across pages; consolidate multi-line entries and ensure consistent indentation and line spacing for readability.
Discussion integrates relevant literature; enhance critical depth by contrasting methodologies of cited studies, noting how they affect comparability with your results.
Strengths are well described; consider quantifying misidentification error by conducting a small validation session to estimate undercount bias.
The application section begins abruptly; include a brief transitional sentence linking your evaluation findings to the proposed low-emission zones.
Proposal for low-emission zones is relevant; add assessment of Beijingâs existing transport policies and potential barriers to implementation to ground the solution locally.
The solution evaluation thoroughly covers pros and cons; consider including a rough cost-benefit estimate to illustrate the economic feasibility of low-emission zones.
